title: Build user onboarding flow
description: Create first-time user experience explaining app features. Include walkthrough for selecting home stations and enabling notifications.
close_reason: Completed user onboarding flow with welcome step, home station selection via GPS, commute setup, and push notification enrollment. Fixed lint errors in migration files.

title: Add express vs local train differentiation
description: Implement train type identification to distinguish express from local trains, enabling better routing decisions and user information. Acceptance criteria: the system correctly identifies train service types and uses this information in routing/display.

title: Implement offline support
description: Add service worker registration, implement caching strategies for core assets, and create offline UI indicators. Acceptance: app works offline for cached content, users see offline status indicators, and service worker updates gracefully.

title: Implement comprehensive testing suite
description: Implement a comprehensive testing suite for the codebase. This should include unit tests, integration tests, and end-to-end tests as appropriate. Ensure tests cover critical paths and edge cases.

title: Implement real-time subway arrivals
description: Fetch and display live subway arrival times from MTA API. Show countdown-style predictions for approaching trains at selected stations.

title: Phase 7: Trust & Resilience - Shuttle bus info, data freshness
description: Add trust and resilience features: real-time shuttle bus information and data freshness indicators.

title: Phase 2: Smart Commute - Transfer analysis, commute presets, express/local
description: Build smart commute features: real-time transfer analysis ('should I transfer?'), commute presets, and express vs local train differentiation.

title: Phase 1: Core - Real-time arrivals, favorites, search, onboarding, complex mapping
description: Implement core features: real-time subway arrivals, favorites management, station search, user onboarding flow, and complex transit mapping.

title: Implement station search
description: Build search functionality to find subway stations by name or line. Filter and display results with station details.

title: Develop context-aware switching
description: Implement intelligent context switching that detects user's current state (commuting, planning, reviewing) and adapts UI accordingly. Use location, time, and usage patterns to determine context.

title: Phase 4: Polish - Offline, accessibility, performance, error states
description: Add polish features: offline support, accessibility improvements (WCAG compliance), performance optimization, and comprehensive error states.

title: Phase 6: Awareness - Elevator status, walking comparison, OMNY, train diagram, health dashboard, annual summary
description: Implement awareness features: elevator/escalator status, walking time comparison, OMNY integration, train diagram visualization, system health dashboard, and annual commute summary.

title: Create complex transit mapping
description: Build interactive map showing subway lines, stations, and real-time train positions. Support zoom, pan, and tap to view station details.

title: Build transfer analysis engine
description: Implement real-time transfer analysis that answers 'should I transfer?' for users. This requires comparing current train status with alternative routes and providing actionable recommendations. Acceptance criteria: users receive transfer recommendations based on real-time data.

title: Add comprehensive error states
description: Implement error boundaries for React components, add retry logic for failed API calls, create user-friendly error messages, and design fallback UIs. Acceptance: no uncaught errors crash the app, all failures have user feedback, and recovery options are clear.

title: Implement commute presets
description: Create a system for users to save, manage, and quickly access their favorite commute routes. Acceptance criteria: users can save common routes and retrieve them for quick trip planning.

title: Implement predictive delay modeling
description: Build delay prediction system using historical MTA data. Analyze patterns in delay frequencies by route, time of day, day of week, and weather conditions. Provide delay probability estimates for trips.

title: Phase 5: Intelligence - Predictive delays, trip tracking, context switching, journal
description: Build intelligence layer: predictive delay modeling, trip tracking with ETA, context-aware switching, and commute journal.

title: Cross-cutting: Testing, security, data migration, observability
description: Implement cross-cutting concerns: comprehensive testing suite, security hardening, data migration tooling, and observability (logging, metrics, tracing).

title: Genesis: MTA My Way Implementation
description:
## Genesis Bead
Tied to plan: /home/coding/mta-my-way/docs/plan/plan.md

## Overview
MTA My Way is a mobile-first PWA for NYC subway commuters. A single container on apexalgo-iad (us-east-1) serves both the Hono API backend and static React frontend through the existing Cloudflare Tunnel. The app provides real-time arrivals, configurable favorites, transfer intelligence, alerts, trip tracking, commute analytics, accessibility features, and system health visualization.

The value proposition is speed-to-information (favorites-first, data in under 3 seconds), transfer intelligence (real-time "should I transfer?" analysis), and alerts that are actually useful (plain-language, filtered to YOUR stations).

## Architecture Decision
Single container on apexalgo-iad was chosen over Cloudflare Workers or Fly.io because:
- Zero new infra cost (rides on existing cluster)
- us-east-1 gives sub-10ms latency to MTA feeds (NYC-hosted)
- Cloudflare Tunnel already configured for public HTTPS access
- GitOps deployment via ArgoCD follows existing patterns
- Same-origin eliminates CORS entirely

## Progress
- [ ] Phase 1: Core — Real-time arrivals, favorites, search, onboarding, complex mapping
- [ ] Phase 2: Smart Commute — Transfer analysis, commute presets, express/local
- [ ] Phase 3: Alerts & Notifications — Alert simplification, push notifications
- [ ] Phase 4: Polish — Offline, accessibility, performance, error states
- [ ] Phase 5: Intelligence — Predictive delays, trip tracking, context switching, journal
- [ ] Phase 6: Awareness — Elevator status, walking comparison, OMNY, train diagram, health dashboard, annual summary
- [ ] Phase 7: Trust & Resilience — Shuttle bus info, data freshness
- [ ] Cross-cutting: Testing, security, data migration, observability

title: Phase 1: Core — Real-Time Arrivals, Favorites, Search
description:
## Goal
A functional app that a commuter can install and use daily for checking arrival times at their stations. This is the foundation everything else builds on.

## Why This Is Phase 1
Every subsequent phase depends on: the backend feed pipeline (polling, parsing, caching), the frontend component architecture (React + Zustand + Tailwind), the PWA shell (Service Worker, manifest), and the GTFS static data (stations, routes, transfers). Nothing works without this.

## Key Decisions
- React 19 + Vite 6 for frontend (component model fits repeating UI patterns, ecosystem depth)
- Hono for backend (lightweight, serves both API and static assets from single process)
- Zustand for state (1.1kB, localStorage persistence middleware, no boilerplate)
- Tailwind CSS 4 for styling (utility-first, smallest CSS bundle, design tokens)
- vite-plugin-pwa for PWA layer (Workbox under the hood)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Backend polls all 8 MTA GTFS-RT feeds every 30 seconds and serves JSON
- Frontend displays arrivals with search, favorites, and auto-refresh
- Station complex mapping resolves all multi-parent-station complexes
- GPS-powered onboarding produces usable dashboard in under 60 seconds
- PWA installable on iOS and Android
- GTFS static data processed into optimized JSON (stations, routes, transfers, complexes)

title: Project scaffold: monorepo, TypeScript, tooling
description:
## What
Initialize the npm workspace monorepo with three packages: shared, server, web. Configure TypeScript, ESLint, Biome, and base tsconfig.

## Why
The monorepo structure enables shared types between frontend and backend (a core architectural decision — end-to-end type safety eliminates a class of bugs where API response shapes diverge from frontend expectations). Tooling must be configured once at the root and inherited by packages.

## Subtasks
- [ ] Initialize npm workspaces in package.json with packages/shared, packages/server, packages/web
- [ ] Create tsconfig.base.json with strict mode, path aliases for @shared/*
- [ ] Configure ESLint + Biome at root level
- [ ] Create .gitignore (node_modules, dist, .env, *.db)
- [ ] Add .nvmrc pinning Node.js 22 LTS
- [ ] Verify `npm install` and `tsc --noEmit` pass from root

## Considerations
- Use npm workspaces (not pnpm/yarn) for simplicity — single lock file, no extra tooling
- tsconfig paths must resolve @shared/* in both server and web packages
- Biome handles formatting (fast), ESLint handles linting (thorough) — no overlap

title: Frontend: station search with type-ahead
description:
## What
Type-ahead search over all 472 subway stations. Search by name, line, or cross-street.

## Subtasks
- [ ] StationSearch component: input field with debounced type-ahead (200ms)
- [ ] SearchResults component: list of matching stations with lines served and borough
- [ ] SearchScreen: search input + results, tap result → StationScreen
- [ ] Client-side search using station index (loaded once, cached by Service Worker)
- [ ] Search handles common abbreviations and partial matches
- [ ] Complex-aware results: searching "Times Square" shows all lines across the complex
- [ ] Empty state: "No stations found" with suggestion to try different terms

## Considerations
- Client-side search is preferred over API search for instant responsiveness
- Station index is small (~472 entries × ~200 bytes = ~94KB) — fine to hold in memory
- Search scoring: exact name match > prefix match > substring match > line match

title: Frontend: GPS-powered onboarding flow
description:
## What
First-time user experience: detect location, suggest nearest stations as favorites, ask about commute destination. Done in 60 seconds.

## Why
Without this, new users see an empty dashboard. The entire favorites-first architecture fails on first open. This flow must produce a useful, personalized dashboard with minimal friction.

## Subtasks
- [ ] OnboardingFlow component: multi-step flow (location → stations → commute)
- [ ] NearbyStations step: request geolocation, find 3 nearest, present as swipeable cards
- [ ] CommuteSetup step: "Where do you commute to?" with station search, auto-create commute
- [ ] Skip option at every step (some users prefer manual setup)
- [ ] Store onboardingComplete flag in localStorage
- [ ] GPS denied fallback: show station search with "Add your first station" guidance card
- [ ] Haversine distance calculation to find nearest stations from coordinates

## Considerations
- Geolocation permission request must include a clear explanation of WHY ("to find stations near you")
- If GPS returns coordinates but no nearby stations (user is not in NYC), show search instead
- The onboarding creates REAL favorites — not a separate "suggested" state. User can delete them immediately
- Must work on both iOS Safari and Android Chrome

title: Dockerfile: multi-stage build for single container
description:
## What
Multi-stage Dockerfile: build web (vite build), build server (tsc), combine into slim Node.js 22 runtime image.

## Subtasks
- [ ] Stage 1 (build-web): npm ci, vite build → /app/packages/web/dist/
- [ ] Stage 2 (build-server): npm ci, tsc → /app/packages/server/dist/
- [ ] Stage 3 (runtime): node:22-slim, copy web dist + server dist, EXPOSE 3000
- [ ] Hono static middleware serves /* from web dist, /api/* from server routes
- [ ] .dockerignore: node_modules, .git, docs, test fixtures
- [ ] Health check: HEALTHCHECK CMD curl -f http://localhost:3000/api/health || exit 1
- [ ] Build and test locally: docker build + docker run + verify arrivals endpoint

## Considerations
- Use node:22-slim (not alpine) for better-sqlite3 compatibility (native addon)
- Multi-stage keeps final image small (~150MB vs ~800MB for full node image)
- GTFS static JSON files are baked into the image at build time
- The container must start polling MTA feeds immediately on startup

title: ArgoCD manifests: namespace, deployment, service, tunnel
description:
## What
Kubernetes manifests for deploying to apexalgo-iad via ArgoCD. In the ardenone-cluster repo at cluster-configuration/apexalgo-iad/mta-my-way/.

## Subtasks
- [ ] namespace.yaml: mta-my-way namespace
- [ ] deployment.yaml: single replica, resource limits, liveness/readiness probes
- [ ] service.yaml: ClusterIP on port 3000
- [ ] ingress.yaml: Cloudflare Tunnel route configuration for public domain
- [ ] pvc.yaml: PersistentVolumeClaim for SQLite (push subscriptions, alert history)
- [ ] kustomization.yaml: Kustomize overlay
- [ ] ArgoCD Application definition pointing to these manifests

## Considerations
- Readiness probe: GET /api/health, returns 503 when 3+ feeds failing >5 min
- Liveness probe: TCP check on port 3000
- Resource limits: start conservative (256Mi memory, 250m CPU), adjust based on actual usage
- PVC size: 1Gi is more than enough for SQLite databases
- The Cloudflare Tunnel route must be configured in the existing tunnel config (not a new tunnel)
- ArgoCD watches this directory and auto-syncs on changes

title: GitHub Actions: CI pipeline + container build
description:
## What
Two GitHub Actions workflows: ci.yml (lint, type-check, test on PRs) and build-push.yml (build and push container image on main).

## Subtasks
- [x] ci.yml: triggered on PR, runs lint (ESLint + Biome), tsc --noEmit, vitest run
- [x] build-push.yml: triggered on push to main, builds multi-stage Dockerfile, pushes to GHCR
- [x] Image tagging: use git SHA as tag (ArgoCD can watch for new tags)
- [x] Cache: npm ci cache, Docker layer cache for faster builds

## Considerations
- CI must pass before image build — tests gate deployment
- GHCR (GitHub Container Registry) is free for public repos
- ArgoCD image updater watches GHCR for new tags and updates the deployment manifest

title: Shared types package: all TypeScript interfaces
description:
## What
Create the packages/shared package with all TypeScript interfaces defined in the plan: StationArrivals, ArrivalTime, Favorite, Commute, Settings, Station, Route, TransferConnection, StationAlert, UserPreferences, EquipmentStatus, FareTracking, TripRecord, CommuteStats, etc.

## Why
These types are the contract between frontend and backend. Defining them first means both sides can develop in parallel against the same interfaces. Changes to the API shape are caught at compile time, not at runtime.

## Subtasks
- [ ] types/arrivals.ts — StationArrivals, ArrivalTime, StationAlert
- [ ] types/favorites.ts — Favorite, Commute, Settings, UserPreferences, FavoriteTapEvent
- [ ] types/stations.ts — Station, Route, TransferConnection, StationIndex, RouteIndex
- [ ] types/alerts.ts — StationAlert (including source: "official" | "predicted")
- [ ] types/trips.ts — TripRecord, CommuteStats
- [ ] types/equipment.ts — EquipmentStatus
- [ ] types/fare.ts — FareTracking, RideLogEntry
- [ ] types/positions.ts — TrainPosition for live diagram
- [ ] constants/feeds.ts — Feed URLs, polling intervals
- [ ] constants/lines.ts — Line metadata (color, division, text color)
- [ ] utils/time.ts — Time formatting, minutes-away calculation
- [ ] utils/confidence.ts — Confidence scoring (division + assigned → high/medium/low)
- [ ] utils/walking.ts — Haversine distance, walking time
- [ ] utils/carbon.ts — CO2 savings computation

## Considerations
- All types should use strict TypeScript (no `any`, no implicit returns)
- Line colors must match official MTA palette exactly (from routes.txt route_color field)
- The confidence scoring logic is shared because both backend (transfer engine buffer) and frontend (display) use it

comments.author: coding
comments.text: All TypeScript types, constants, and utilities implemented in packages/shared. Commit: 3765cc4

title: GTFS static data pipeline: download, parse, process
description:
## What
Build the script that downloads MTA GTFS static data (stops.txt, routes.txt, transfers.txt, stop_times.txt) and processes it into optimized JSON files: stations.json, routes.json, transfers.json, travel-times.json, complexes.json.

## Why
This is the foundation for ALL station-level features. Station search, favorites display, transfer engine, walking comparison, complex mapping — everything needs this processed data. It runs at build time (baked into the container image) and periodically when MTA updates schedules.

## Subtasks
- [ ] Download script: fetch gtfs_subway.zip and gtfs_supplemented.zip from MTA S3
- [ ] Parse stops.txt → stations.json (parent station grouping, lat/lon, lines, borough, ADA status)
- [ ] Parse routes.txt → routes.json (id, color, textColor, division, feedId)
- [ ] Parse transfers.txt → transfers.json (transfer connections with walking times)
- [ ] Parse stop_times.txt → travel-times.json (inter-station travel times per route)
- [ ] Station complex mapping: combine MTA Station Complexes CSV + parent_station field → complexes.json
- [ ] Create complex-overrides.json for ~5 edge cases (Fulton St/Broadway-Nassau, etc.)
- [ ] Verify all 472 stations are present and correctly grouped
- [ ] Add npm script: `npm run process-gtfs` in packages/server

## Considerations
- The MTA Station Complexes CSV is published on the MTA open data portal (data.ny.gov)
- stop_times.txt is large (~30MB). Only extract inter-station travel times, not full schedules
- Travel times should be indexed by route_id + direction for efficient lookup
- The supplemented feed includes 7-day service changes; prefer it over base schedule
- This script should be idempotent — safe to re-run when MTA updates data

title: Protobuf compilation: GTFS-RT + NYCT extensions
description:
## What
Obtain gtfs-realtime.proto and nyct-subway.proto, compile them into a JavaScript module using protobufjs.

## Why
MTA feeds return binary protobuf, not JSON. The NYCT extensions (direction, is_assigned, train_id, scheduled_track, actual_track, trip_replacement_period) are critical for confidence scoring, reroute detection, and cancelled trip detection. Without the compiled proto, we can only parse base GTFS-RT fields.

## Subtasks
- [ ] Obtain gtfs-realtime.proto from MobilityData/gtfs-realtime-bindings
- [ ] Obtain nyct-subway.proto from MTA developer resources
- [ ] Compile both into packages/server/src/proto/compiled.js using protobufjs CLI (pbjs)
- [ ] Generate TypeScript definitions (pbts) for type safety
- [ ] Verify parsing of a sample MTA feed response
- [ ] Add npm script: `npm run compile-proto` in packages/server

## Considerations
- nyct-subway.proto extends the base GTFS-RT message types with MTA-specific fields
- The compiled output should be checked into git (not generated at build time) to avoid protobufjs as a build dependency
- Test with a real downloaded feed binary to catch extension parsing issues early

title: Backend: feed poller, parser, transformer, cache
description:
## What
Build the core backend: Hono app that polls all 8 MTA GTFS-RT feeds every 30 seconds, parses protobuf (including NYCT extensions), transforms into StationArrivals model, and caches in memory.

## Why
This is the beating heart of the entire application. Every feature — arrivals, transfers, alerts, delay detection, trip tracking, train positions — depends on this pipeline running correctly every 30 seconds.

## Subtasks
- [ ] Hono app setup (app.ts): routes, middleware, static asset serving config
- [ ] Feed poller (poller.ts): setInterval(30s), fetch all 8 feeds in parallel with Promise.allSettled
- [ ] Feed parser (parser.ts): protobuf decode using compiled proto, extract NYCT extensions
- [ ] Feed transformer (transformer.ts): convert parsed data → StationArrivals indexed by stopId
- [ ] In-memory cache (cache.ts): 30s TTL, fallback to last-good response on fetch failure, mark stale after 5 min
- [ ] Circuit breaker: after 3 consecutive failures on a feed, pause 60s before retrying
- [ ] API routes: GET /api/arrivals/:stationId, GET /api/stations, GET /api/stations/:id
- [ ] Health endpoint: GET /api/health with per-feed status
- [ ] Structured JSON logging on every poll (timestamp, feed, status, latency, entities, errors)
- [ ] Static asset serving: Hono serves /* from web dist directory, /api/* from routes
- [ ] Entry point (index.ts): start server, start poller, log startup info

## Considerations
- Use Promise.allSettled (not Promise.all) so one feed failure doesn't block others
- Each feed has its own circuit breaker state — independent failure isolation
- The cache must serve stale data rather than nothing when feeds are down
- Per-feed timestamps must be tracked for the data freshness feature (Phase 7)
- Memory usage: ~8 feeds × ~500 entities × 30s = manageable in-memory, no external cache needed
- The poller should start polling immediately on startup (not wait 30s for first poll)

title: Backend: station search and static data API
description:
## What
API endpoints for station search and serving pre-processed GTFS static data. These serve the frontend's search, station detail, and commute configuration features.

## Subtasks
- [ ] GET /api/stations — return full station index (from stations.json), cached aggressively
- [ ] GET /api/stations/:id — return single station with complex-aware expansion (all stations in same complex)
- [ ] GET /api/stations/search?q=... — type-ahead search by name, line, or cross-street
- [ ] GET /api/routes — return route index
- [ ] GET /api/static/complexes — return complexes.json
- [ ] Cache-Control headers: max-age=86400 for static data (changes only a few times per year)
- [ ] Complex-aware station lookup: when querying a station, include all lines across the complex

## Considerations
- Search should be fast (in-memory, not SQLite) since station count is only 472
- Search should handle common abbreviations: "Times Sq", "42 St", "B'way"
- Complex expansion is critical — searching "Times Square" must show 1/2/3/7/N/Q/R/W/S

title: Frontend: React + Vite + PWA shell setup
description:
## What
Set up the frontend package: React 19, Vite 6, vite-plugin-pwa, Tailwind CSS 4, React Router 7, Zustand. Create the PWA manifest, Service Worker configuration, and base layout components.

## Subtasks
- [ ] Initialize packages/web with Vite + React + TypeScript template
- [ ] Install and configure: vite-plugin-pwa, tailwindcss, react-router, zustand
- [ ] PWA manifest (manifest.json): app name, icons (192, 512, maskable), theme color (#0039A6), standalone display
- [ ] Service Worker config in vite.config.ts (Workbox strategies for static assets)
- [ ] Tailwind config: MTA color palette, system font stack, spacing scale, dark mode
- [ ] Base layout: Screen wrapper, Header, BottomNav (4 tabs: Home, Search, Commute, Alerts)
- [ ] React Router setup with lazy-loaded routes per screen
- [ ] API client (lib/api.ts): fetch wrapper with base URL, error handling, type-safe responses
- [ ] Global styles (globals.css): Tailwind imports, CSS reset, safe area insets for mobile

## Considerations
- Bottom nav is FIXED — thumbs reach the bottom, not the top. Must not scroll away.
- Touch targets minimum 44x44px on all interactive elements
- System font (system-ui) for zero loading cost
- Dark mode support from day one via Tailwind's dark: variant + prefers-color-scheme
- Safe area insets (env(safe-area-inset-bottom)) for notched phones

title: Frontend: Zustand stores with versioned persistence
description:
## What
Create all Zustand stores with localStorage persistence, versioned migration support, and backup safety.

## Why
This implements the data migration strategy (Section 14 of plan) from the start, not as a retrofit. Every store created from day one uses versioned persist, so schema evolution is safe throughout the entire project lifecycle.

## Subtasks
- [ ] favoritesStore: Favorite[], Commute[], tapHistory[], with persist v1
- [ ] settingsStore: theme, refreshInterval, showUnassignedTrips, accessibleMode, etc.
- [ ] arrivalsStore: cached StationArrivals per stationId (in-memory, not persisted — refresh from API)
- [ ] Migration infrastructure: backup key before migration, restore on failure, Sentry error logging
- [ ] Skeleton journalStore and fareStore (Phase 5-6, but version 1 schema defined now)

## Considerations
- Zustand persist middleware's version + migrate options handle the chain
- Backup key format: _mta_backup_{storeName}_v{version}
- tapHistory capped at 500 entries FIFO — must enforce on every append
- arrivalsStore is NOT persisted (it's a cache, refreshed every 30s) — but last-known values should be written to localStorage separately for offline mode

title: Frontend: home dashboard, favorites, arrivals display
description:
## What
The core user-facing screens: home dashboard showing favorites with inline arrivals, station detail view, and the favorites management system (add, edit, remove, reorder).

## Why
This is the entire value proposition of the app — "open and see your data in under 3 seconds." The home screen IS the app. Everything else is secondary to this screen working perfectly.

## Subtasks
- [ ] FavoriteCard component: station name, configured lines/direction, next 2-3 arrivals inline
- [ ] FavoritesList component: ordered list of FavoriteCards, drag-to-reorder
- [ ] ArrivalRow component: line bullet (official MTA color), destination, minutes away, confidence indicator
- [ ] ArrivalList component: grouped by direction (Uptown/Downtown with compass-aware labels)
- [ ] LineBullet component: circular, 32px, official MTA color, white/dark text
- [ ] ConfidenceBar component: solid (high), dashed (medium), dotted (low)
- [ ] HomeScreen: favorites list + "Updated Xs ago" indicator + pull-to-refresh
- [ ] StationScreen: full station detail with all arrivals, both directions, add-to-favorites button
- [ ] FavoriteEditor: configure lines and direction per favorite
- [ ] useArrivals hook: fetch arrivals for a station, auto-refresh every 30s, return DataState
- [ ] useFavorites hook: CRUD operations on favorites store
- [ ] Pull-to-refresh: trigger immediate API fetch, optional haptic on iOS
- [ ] DataState wrapper on all data views (loading skeleton, empty, error, stale, offline)

## Considerations
- Arrival time is the HERO number — 24px, 800 weight, the largest element on screen
- Line bullets must be 44px tap target (32px visible + padding)
- Direction labels should say "Uptown / Bronx-bound" not just "Northbound" — context matters
- Multi-complex stations (Times Square) show a link to the other platform group
- DataState skeleton should match the shape of the actual content (not a generic spinner)

title: Phase 2: Smart Commute — Transfer Analysis, Trip Planning
description:
## Goal
Help commuters answer "should I transfer?" and "what is the fastest way to get to my destination right now?"

## Why This Matters
The competitive analysis shows NO existing app answers the transfer question with real-time data. Subway Now shows arrivals; Citymapper plans trips. Neither tells you "if you transfer to the express at this station, you save 4 minutes based on current train positions."

## Key Decisions
- Transfer engine uses station graph from transfers.txt + real-time arrival data
- Express/local detection from analyzing stop patterns in GTFS-RT feed
- B Division lines get +2 min buffer in transfer calculations (80-95% accuracy vs A Division)
- Commute dashboard cards show direct vs transfer comparison inline on home screen

## Acceptance Criteria
- Transfer engine computes multi-leg routes from real-time data
- Commute cards on home screen show direct vs transfer comparison with "Transfer saves X min" badge
- Express/local detection works for Manhattan trunk lines
- B Division confidence indicators displayed (solid vs dashed vs dotted)

## Dependencies
- Requires Phase 1 backend (feed pipeline, station data) and frontend (component architecture)

title: Transfer engine: graph, computation, ranking
description:
## What
Build the transfer analysis engine that computes multi-leg routes from real-time arrival data for a given origin/destination pair.

## Subtasks
- [ ] Station graph (graph.ts): build weighted graph from transfers.json + complexes.json
- [ ] Travel times (travel-times.ts): load scheduled inter-station times, index by route+direction
- [ ] Engine (engine.ts): for origin→destination, find all direct routes and transfer routes
- [ ] Direct route scoring: line + next arrival at origin + travel time to destination
- [ ] Transfer route scoring: first leg + walking time + wait at transfer + second leg travel time
- [ ] B Division buffer: add +2 min to B Division arrival estimates in transfer calculations
- [ ] Rank all routes by total estimated arrival time at destination
- [ ] Flag when transfer saves time vs direct: "Transfer saves X min" computation
- [ ] API route: POST /api/commute/analyze (origin, destination, preferred lines)

## Considerations
- Transfer routes are limited to 1 transfer (2 legs max) — 2+ transfers are rarely useful in NYC
- The graph must include complex-aware transfers (e.g., 1/2/3 → N/Q/R/W at Times Square)
- Walking time defaults to transfers.txt min_transfer_time, fallback 180s
- Express/local detection: compare a trip's stop_time_updates against the route's full stop list — missing stops = express

title: Frontend: commute configuration and dashboard cards
description:
## What
Frontend for configuring commutes and displaying transfer analysis on the home dashboard.

## Subtasks
- [ ] CommuteEditor: origin/destination station pickers, preferred lines, save up to 10 commutes
- [ ] CommuteCard: home screen card showing best direct + best transfer with "Transfer saves X min" badge
- [ ] CommuteScreen: full commute planner with RECOMMENDED, DIRECT, and ALSO POSSIBLE sections
- [ ] TransferDetail: expandable view showing each leg, wait times, estimated arrival
- [ ] RouteComparison: side-by-side direct vs transfer
- [ ] useCommute hook: fetch commute analysis, auto-refresh
- [ ] Express/local indicator on arrival rows (when detected)
- [ ] B Division confidence display: solid (A Division) vs dashed (B Division) indicators with tooltip

title: Phase 3: Alerts & Notifications — Service Alerts, Push
description:
## Goal
Proactively inform commuters about disruptions to their specific stations and lines, and help them respond. Alert text is simplified from MTA's confusing language into plain English.

## Why This Matters
The competitive analysis documents that MTA notifications are either too broad (line-level) or incomprehensible (raw MTA alert text). This is a key differentiator.

## Key Decisions
- Template-based alert rewriter with ~15 regex patterns covering recurring MTA alert formats
- Graceful fallback: unmatched alerts shown as raw text with "raw alert" indicator
- Web Push API (RFC 8030) with VAPID authentication for push notifications
- Push subscriptions keyed by endpoint hash — no user accounts needed
- Quiet hours (default midnight to 5 AM)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Alerts feed parsed and simplified into plain language (target: 85%+ match rate)
- Filtered alerts displayed in app, scoped to user's favorites
- Web Push notifications delivered for severe alerts
- Trip replacement period detection shows cancelled trips

## Dependencies
- Requires Phase 1 backend (alert feed polling) and Phase 2 (commute configuration for filtering)

title: Alert parser: ingestion, pattern matching, simplification
description:
## What
Parse the MTA subway alerts GTFS-RT feed. Build the template-based alert rewriter with ~15 regex patterns that converts confusing MTA language into plain English.

## Subtasks
- [ ] Alerts poller: fetch subway-alerts feed every 60 seconds
- [ ] Alert parser (alerts-parser.ts): decode protobuf, extract affected lines/stations/periods
- [ ] Create alert-patterns.json: ~15 regex patterns with named capture groups and output templates
- [ ] Pattern matcher: iterate patterns, extract structured data, generate plain English
- [ ] Severity mapping: UNKNOWN_EFFECT→info, SIGNIFICANT_DELAYS→warning, NO_SERVICE→severe
- [ ] Fallback: unmatched alerts shown with raw text + "raw alert" flag
- [ ] Trip replacement period: detect cancelled trips absent from feed within replacement window
- [ ] API routes: GET /api/alerts, GET /api/alerts/:lineId
- [ ] Alert diffing: track previous alerts, detect new/changed/resolved for push notifications
- [ ] Log unmatched alert patterns for growing the pattern library

## Considerations
- Start with 10 most common patterns; grow from production logs of unmatched alerts
- Target: 85%+ alert simplification match rate
- Affected stations must be extracted for filtering to user favorites
- Alert text often contains HTML entities and brackets — strip before pattern matching

title: Frontend: alert display, filtering, banners
description:
## What
Display alerts in the app: dedicated alerts screen, inline banners on station/commute views, badge count, severity color coding.

## Subtasks
- [ ] AlertsScreen: filtered feed (my lines vs all lines toggle), sorted by severity then recency
- [ ] AlertCard: severity color (red/yellow/gray), headline, expandable description, since timestamp
- [ ] AlertBanner: inline banner on StationScreen and CommuteScreen when relevant alerts exist
- [ ] AlertList: used in both AlertsScreen and as embedded list on other screens
- [ ] Badge count on Alerts tab in bottom nav
- [ ] useAlerts hook: fetch alerts, filter by user's favorites, return DataState
- [ ] "raw alert" visual indicator for unmatched patterns (dashed border, muted style)

title: Web Push: subscription, matching, notification delivery
description:
## What
Full Web Push notification pipeline: VAPID key generation, subscription management, alert-to-subscription matching, push message delivery.

## Subtasks
- [x] Generate VAPID key pair, store in environment variables
- [x] Backend: POST /api/push/subscribe — store PushSubscription + user's favorite tuples in SQLite
- [x] Backend: DELETE /api/push/unsubscribe — remove subscription
- [x] Subscription storage (subscriptions.ts): SQLite table keyed by SHA-256 of endpoint
- [x] Alert matcher (matcher.ts): when new/changed alert detected, find matching subscriptions
- [x] Push sender (sender.ts): send Web Push messages using web-push npm package
- [x] Quiet hours: skip notifications between midnight and 5 AM (user-configurable)
- [x] Frontend: usePushNotifications hook — request permission, register subscription, send to backend
- [x] Service Worker: handle 'push' event, display notification even when app is closed
- [x] Frontend: notification settings in SettingsScreen (enable/disable, quiet hours)

## Considerations
- NEVER request push permission on first visit — only when user actively enables in settings
- Push subscriptions contain no PII — keyed by endpoint hash
- iOS Safari requires iOS 16.4+ for Web Push — detect and show appropriate message on older versions
- Web Push is best-effort — critical alerts must also be shown in-app

title: Phase 4: Polish — Offline, Accessibility, Performance
description:
## Goal
Production-quality app that is fast, accessible, and reliable even underground with no signal.

## Why This Matters
Subway commuters are underground most of their transit time. An app that breaks offline is useless exactly when it's needed most. Accessibility is non-negotiable for a public transit app — wheelchair users, visually impaired riders, and elderly commuters all depend on transit.

## Key Decisions
- WCAG 2.1 AA minimum compliance (not just aspirational — tested)
- Lighthouse targets: Performance 95+, Accessibility 100, Best Practices 100, PWA 100
- Stale data detection via VehiclePosition timestamps
- DataState wrapper component enforces 6 states on all data views (Section 13 of plan)
- Bundle size budget enforced in CI

## Acceptance Criteria
- Offline mode shows cached data with staleness indicator
- WCAG 2.1 AA compliance verified by automated and manual testing
- Screen reader tested on VoiceOver (iOS) and TalkBack (Android)
- First Contentful Paint under 1.5s on 3G
- All data views handle loading/loaded/empty/error/stale/offline states via DataState

## Dependencies
- Requires Phases 1-3 features to be functional (this phase polishes them)

title: Offline mode: Service Worker caching, stale data display
description:
## What
Full offline capability: cache last-known arrivals per station, serve cached data with staleness indicator, station search works offline.

## Subtasks
- [ ] Service Worker cache strategy: CacheFirst for static assets, StaleWhileRevalidate for API
- [ ] Write last-known arrivals to localStorage on each successful fetch
- [ ] Offline detection: useOnlineStatus hook wrapping navigator.onLine + online/offline events
- [ ] OfflineBanner component: persistent "You are offline" chip when offline
- [ ] Stale data visualization: data fades after 2 min, grays out after 5 min
- [ ] Station search works offline (station index cached by Service Worker)
- [ ] Background Sync: queue push subscription changes when offline, sync when reconnected

title: Accessibility: WCAG 2.1 AA compliance
description:
## What
Full accessibility audit and implementation: semantic HTML, ARIA, keyboard navigation, contrast, reduced motion.

## Subtasks
- [ ] Semantic HTML: proper heading hierarchy (h1-h3), landmark regions (main, nav, aside)
- [ ] ARIA labels on all interactive elements, line bullets, status indicators
- [ ] aria-live="polite" regions for arrival time updates
- [ ] aria-live="assertive" for new alerts
- [ ] Keyboard navigation: all interactive elements focusable, tab order logical
- [ ] Focus management: screen transitions move focus to heading, modals trap focus
- [ ] Color contrast: all text 4.5:1 against background, large text 3:1
- [ ] Touch targets: minimum 44x44px verified on all interactive elements
- [ ] Text sizing: all rem units, tested at 200% zoom
- [ ] prefers-reduced-motion: instant transitions, simple pull-to-refresh indicator
- [ ] Line color is never the only indicator — letter/number always accompanies color
- [ ] Alert severity uses icons (triangle, octagon) in addition to color
- [ ] Test with VoiceOver (iOS Safari) and TalkBack (Android Chrome)

title: Performance: bundle budget, code splitting, Lighthouse 95+
description:
## What
Optimize for performance: route-based code splitting, bundle size budget, compression, FCP under 1.5s on 3G.

## Subtasks
- [x] Route-based code splitting: lazy load all screens except HomeScreen
- [x] Bundle size budget: CI fails if any chunk >50KB gzipped or total JS >150KB gzipped
- [x] rollup-plugin-visualizer in build to track chunk sizes
- [x] SVG line bullets (no raster images)
- [x] API response compression: gzip/brotli via Hono middleware
- [x] Static asset immutable caching: Cache-Control max-age=31536000 for hashed filenames
- [x] Lighthouse CI: run in GitHub Actions, fail build if Performance <95
- [x] FCP measurement: test on simulated 3G via Lighthouse, target <1.5s

title: DataState wrapper: universal error/empty/loading states
description:
## What
Implement the DataState component that enforces loading/loaded/empty/error/stale/offline states on all data views (Section 13 of plan).

## Subtasks
- [ ] DataState component: accepts state enum, renders appropriate UI per state
- [ ] SkeletonCard: animated shimmer placeholder shaped like FavoriteCard
- [ ] Skeleton variants for ArrivalList, AlertList, CommuteCard
- [ ] Empty state messages: contextual per screen (no favorites, no arrivals, no alerts)
- [ ] Error state: plain-language message + retry button
- [ ] Stale state: amber banner with "Updated X min ago"
- [ ] Audit all screens to ensure DataState wraps every data display

## Considerations
- This should be created early in Phase 4 and retroactively applied to Phase 1-3 screens
- Skeletons must match the SHAPE of actual content — not generic rectangles

title: Phase 5: Intelligence — Predictive Delays, Trip Tracking, Learning
description:
## Goal
Transform the app from a data display into a predictive, context-aware commute companion that learns and anticipates.

## Why This Matters
This is where the app becomes genuinely differentiated. No competitor predicts delays before the MTA announces them, no competitor tracks your trip and shares ETA, no competitor learns your patterns to auto-sort favorites, and no competitor builds a personal commute history.

## Key Decisions
- Predictive delay detection: diff VehiclePosition across polls, compare vs scheduled inter-station times
- Trip tracking locks onto a specific tripId in the GTFS-RT feed
- Time-aware context switching is 100% client-side (localStorage tapHistory)
- Smart pre-fetch uses Geolocation API (only works when app is open — acceptable)
- Commute journal infers trips from app usage patterns (no always-on required)
- OMNY fare cap auto-logged from trip data (no manual button)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Predictive delay warnings fire 5-10 min before official MTA alerts for detectable slowdowns
- Live trip tracking with shareable ETA link working
- Context-aware favorite sorting active after 2 weeks of usage
- Offline countdown working underground with cached data
- Commute journal logging trips and surfacing anomalies

## Dependencies
- Requires Phase 1-2 (backend feed pipeline, transfer engine, station data)
- Requires Phase 3 (push infrastructure for morning briefing notifications)
close_reason: Phase 5 Intelligence complete. Predictive delay detection, live trip tracking, context-aware sorting, offline PWA, commute journal. All 296 tests passing.

title: Predictive delay detection from VehiclePosition diffs
description:
## What
Detect delays before MTA announces them by tracking VehiclePosition changes across consecutive polls. When trains are stopped or moving abnormally slowly, fire a synthetic early-warning alert.

## Subtasks
- [ ] delay-detector.ts: track each tripId position across consecutive 30s polls
- [ ] Compute actual inter-station traversal time vs scheduled baseline from travel-times.json
- [ ] Single-train alert: when actual > 1.5x scheduled for a segment, flag that segment
- [ ] Line-level alert: when 2+ trains on same line show slowdowns, escalate to line alert
- [ ] Synthetic alert generation: create StationAlert with source: "predicted", distinct severity
- [ ] Expose via GET /api/alerts with source field so frontend can display distinctly (amber dotted border)
- [ ] Tuning: configurable threshold multiplier (default 1.5x) and minimum sample count (2 trains)

## Considerations
- A Division (ATS) will produce much better predictions than B Division (Bluetooth beacons)
- False positives are worse than false negatives — start conservative (2.0x threshold) and tune down
- Terminal stations will show trains "stopped" for extended periods — exclude first/last stops
close_reason:
Predictive delay detection from VehiclePosition diffs - frontend display component.

The backend delay detection was already fully implemented:
- Tracks tripId positions across consecutive 30s polls
- Computes actual inter-station traversal time vs scheduled baseline
- Generates single-train alerts when actual > 2x scheduled (configurable)
- Escalates to line-level alerts when 2+ trains show slowdowns
- Creates StationAlert with source: predicted and appropriate severity
- Exposes via GET /api/alerts with source field

Frontend changes:
- Added isPredicted prop to AlertCard with amber dotted border styling
- AlertList passes isPredicted based on alert.source === predicted
- AlertBanner shows (predicted) label for predicted alerts

Committed: feat(alerts): display predicted alerts with amber dotted border

title: Live trip tracker with shareable ETA link
description:
## What
"I am on this train" feature: lock onto a tripId, show stop-by-stop progress, countdown to destination, shareable URL.

## Subtasks
- [ ] API route: GET /api/trip/:tripId — return stop_time_updates for this trip
- [ ] Frontend: TripTracker component with vertical timeline (passed/next/upcoming/destination)
- [ ] "I am on this train" button on ArrivalRow in StationScreen
- [ ] TripScreen: full-screen trip progress view with ETA as hero number
- [ ] ETA sharing: generate URL (mtamyway.com/trip/:tripId), store destination in URL params
- [ ] TripSharePage: lightweight read-only page that polls /api/trip/:tripId
- [ ] Share links expire naturally when tripId leaves the GTFS-RT feed
- [ ] useTripTracker hook: poll trip endpoint every 30s, manage trip state

## Considerations
- Trip IDs are not stable across days — they only exist while the trip is active in the feed
- When underground, use cached last-known position + timer-based estimation
- "Stop tracking" button must be prominent — easy to exit trip mode
close_reason: The live trip tracker with shareable ETA link feature was already fully implemented. Fixed TypeScript errors in trip-lookup.ts and a bug in deriveStops where destination stop could get wrong status. All subtasks verified: API route, TripTracker component, "I'm on this train" button, TripScreen with ETA hero, shareable URLs, 24h TTL, useTripTracker hook.

title: Time-aware context switching (client-side)
description:
## What
Auto-sort favorites based on which ones the user taps at different times/days. Entirely client-side.

## Subtasks
- [ ] On every favorite tap, append {favoriteId, dayOfWeek, hour} to tapHistory (max 500, FIFO)
- [ ] useContextSort hook: on app open, score each favorite by frequency at current day+hour (±1h window)
- [ ] Re-sort favorites by score, with manual sortOrder as tiebreaker
- [ ] "Pinned" favorites override auto-sort
- [ ] Morning briefing: compose status text for top morning favorites, trigger push via Phase 3 infra

## Considerations
- Needs ~2 weeks of data before sorting becomes meaningful — don't sort with <20 tap events
- The scoring is a simple frequency count, not ML — keep it predictable and debuggable

title: Smart underground pre-fetch via Geolocation
description:
## What
When approaching a station, aggressively cache arrival data for all stations on the user's commute routes. Timer-based countdown while offline underground.

## Subtasks
- [ ] useGeofence hook: watchPosition, detect when user enters 200m radius of any station
- [ ] On geofence trigger: batch-fetch arrivals for all stations on user's commute routes
- [ ] Cache responses in Service Worker Cache API (separate from normal API caching)
- [ ] Timer-based countdown: use cached arrival times + travel-times.json for offline estimation
- [ ] Label timer-based estimates as "estimated" — refresh to live data on connectivity return
- [ ] Battery-conscious: enableHighAccuracy=false, stop watching when GPS fails (underground)
- [ ] prefetch.ts library: manage pre-fetch triggers and cache lifecycle
close_reason:
Smart Underground Pre-fetch via Geolocation - Complete

All subtasks were already implemented:

1. useGeofence hook - watchPosition, 200m radius detection, stops after 3 GPS failures
2. usePrefetch hook - batch-fetches arrivals for favorites + commute stations
3. prefetch.ts library - Cache API storage with 10-min TTL, dedup
4. useOfflineCountdown - timer-based countdown from cached arrivals, marked as estimated
5. ArrivalRow shows EST badge for estimated arrivals
6. Battery-conscious: enableHighAccuracy=false

Additional: Added morning scores for personalized push notifications.

title: Commute journal with anomaly detection
description:
## What
Automatically log commute trips. Build personal travel history. Detect when a trip is taking longer than usual.

## Subtasks
- [ ] journalStore: TripRecord[] (last 90 days, cap 500), CommuteStats per commute
- [ ] Auto-log from trip tracker: on trip complete, log {origin, destination, departure, arrival, duration, line, source:"tracked"}
- [ ] Inferred trips: when app opens at station A then later at station B (downstream, same line), log with source:"inferred"
- [ ] CommuteStats computation: rolling average, median, stdDev for each commute + day-of-week
- [ ] Anomaly detection: current duration > mean + 1.5*stdDev → banner "This trip is 6 min longer than usual"
- [ ] Weekly digest push notification: average duration, trend %, worst day
- [ ] JournalScreen: trip history list, duration chart (sparkline), stats summary

title: Phase 6: Awareness — Accessibility, Walking, Visualization, Stats
description:
## Goal
Make the app aware of the physical world — elevator outages, weather, walking alternatives, and train positions — so it gives smarter, more humane recommendations.

## Key Decisions
- MTA Equipment API polled every 5 min for elevator/escalator status
- Accessible mode in transfer engine sets broken-elevator stations to weight Infinity
- Walking comparison uses Haversine distance from GTFS static coordinates
- OMNY tracker auto-logs from commute journal (Phase 5), no manual tap button
- Train position diagram is SVG schematic (not geographic map)
- Annual summary rendered client-side via html2canvas, shared via Web Share API

## Acceptance Criteria
- Elevator/escalator outages displayed; accessible rerouting avoids broken stations
- Walking comparison surfaces for short trips and during delays
- OMNY fare cap tracker counting rides from auto-logged data
- Live train position diagram rendering for all lines
- System health dashboard showing all-line status
- Annual summary generating shareable cards

## Dependencies
- Requires Phase 5 (commute journal for OMNY auto-logging, trip tracking)
- Requires Phase 1 (VehiclePosition data for train diagram)

title: Elevator/escalator status + accessible rerouting
description:
## What
Integrate MTA Equipment API for real-time elevator/escalator outages. In accessible mode, transfer engine avoids broken-elevator stations.

## Subtasks
- [ ] equipment-poller.ts: poll MTA Equipment API every 5 minutes
- [ ] Parse outage data, index by stationId
- [ ] API route: GET /api/equipment/:stationId and GET /api/equipment (bulk)
- [ ] Inject equipment status into StationArrivals response
- [ ] Frontend: EquipmentBadge on station cards (red badge when elevator out)
- [ ] Frontend: EquipmentBanner on StationScreen with outage details
- [ ] Settings: accessibleMode toggle (persisted in settingsStore)
- [ ] Transfer engine: when accessibleMode=true, weight broken-elevator stations as Infinity
- [ ] useEquipment hook: fetch equipment status for a station

title: Walking vs transit comparison for short trips
description:
## What
For trips of 1-3 stops, show walking time comparison. Promote walking when it's faster than waiting during delays.

## Subtasks
- [ ] useWalkComparison hook: compute walking time from Haversine distance at 4.5 km/h
- [ ] WalkComparison component: side-by-side "Walk 11 min vs wait 6 + ride 2 min"
- [ ] Show on CommuteScreen when commute is ≤3 stops AND walking time <20 min
- [ ] During delays: promote walking when wait time > walking time (walking icon, green highlight)
- [ ] Shared util: walking.ts (already in shared types package)
close_reason:
Walking vs transit comparison for short trips — fully implemented:

- Shared util: walking.ts with haversine distance, 4.5 km/h pace, compareWalkingVsTransit, isWalkingViable
- useWalkComparison hook: computes walking time from station coords, compares against best transit route
- WalkComparison component: side-by-side display with green highlight when walking is faster
- Integrated into CommuteScreen detail view for commutes ≤3 stops with <20 min walk time
- Delay promotion: shows 'Long wait — skip the platform' when wait time exceeds walking time

title: OMNY fare cap tracker (auto-logged)
description:
## What
Track rides toward OMNY's 12-ride weekly free cap. Auto-logged from commute journal — no manual button.

## Subtasks
- [ ] fareStore: weeklyRides, weekStartDate, monthlyRides, rideLog[], currentFare ($2.90)
- [ ] Auto-log: when journalStore records a trip, increment ride count and append rideLog entry
- [ ] Weekly reset: when current Monday differs from weekStartDate, reset counter
- [ ] FareTracker component: "9/12 rides — 3 more until free" progress indicator
- [ ] Monthly comparison: rides × fare vs $132 unlimited pass
- [ ] Nudge: when at 10-11 rides, show "Take one more round trip for free rides"

title: Live train position diagram (SVG)
description:
## What
SVG schematic showing actual train positions as dots on a linear station-to-station representation per line.

## Subtasks
- [ ] API route: GET /api/positions/:lineId — return VehiclePosition entries for a route
- [ ] Frontend: TrainDiagram component — SVG line with station nodes as circles
- [ ] TrainDot component: colored dot interpolated between stations using current_stop_sequence + current_status
- [ ] Dot interpolation: INCOMING_AT=90% to next station, STOPPED_AT=at station, IN_TRANSIT_TO=between stations
- [ ] Highlight user's next train (pulsing animation, respects prefers-reduced-motion)
- [ ] Tap a dot: show trip details (destination, assigned, delay)
- [ ] usePositions hook: fetch positions every 30s
- [ ] Accessible: screen reader announces train count, spacing summary, next train distance

## Considerations
- Station ordering from stop_times.txt stop_sequence per route
- NOT a geographic map — a schematic line diagram (think London Tube map style)
- Bunched trains visible at a glance (dots clustered = service problem)

title: System-wide health dashboard
description:
## What
Single screen showing all 26 subway lines with status: normal/minor delays/significant delays/suspended.

## Subtasks
- [ ] HealthScreen: grid of all line bullets with status color
- [ ] LineStatus component: line bullet + one-line summary for affected lines
- [ ] Overall health percentage: (normal lines / total lines) × 100
- [ ] Status aggregation from: official alerts (Phase 3) + predictive delays (Phase 5)
- [ ] Tap any line → jump to line detail (arrivals + position diagram)
- [ ] Status tiers: green (normal), yellow (minor), orange (significant), red (suspended)
- [ ] No new API calls — purely frontend aggregation of existing /api/alerts data

title: 'Your Subway Year' shareable annual summary
description:
## What
Personalized year-in-review from commute journal + fare tracker. Shareable as PNG card.

## Subtasks
- [ ] StatsScreen: configurable time window (this month, quarter, year, all time)
- [ ] Compute: total trips, hours underground, distance, most-used station/line, delays, streaks
- [ ] Carbon savings: total_miles × 374g CO2/mi saved (EPA: car 404g - subway 30g = 374g delta)
- [ ] SubwayYear component: styled summary card
- [ ] ShareCard: render to PNG via html2canvas
- [ ] Share button: navigator.share() for native mobile sharing
- [ ] Entirely client-side — computed from journalStore + fareStore in localStorage

title: Phase 7: Trust & Resilience — Shuttle Info, Data Freshness
description:
## Goal
Build user trust through data transparency and provide critical fallback information when the system breaks down.

## Why This Matters
The worst moment for a commuter is when their line is suspended. The MTA says "use shuttle bus" but never says WHERE. And users need to know how fresh the data they're seeing actually is — especially given the A Division vs B Division accuracy gap.

## Key Decisions
- Shuttle bus stops are a curated static JSON lookup (not dynamic — MTA doesn't provide this data electronically)
- Start with 10 most common suspension segments, grow incrementally
- Data freshness shows per-feed age with color coding: green <15s, amber 45-90s, red >90s
- Per-arrival feedName field lets frontend show which feed each arrival came from

## Acceptance Criteria
- Shuttle bus info displayed for 10+ common suspension segments
- Data freshness visible on all station detail views with color-coded staleness

## Dependencies
- Requires Phase 3 (alert parsing for NO_SERVICE detection)
- Requires Phase 1 (feed pipeline with per-feed timestamps)
close_reason: Phase 7 complete: Shuttle info for 10 segments, data freshness with color-coded feed ages (green<15s, amber 45-90s, red>90s), integrated into StationScreen

title: Shuttle bus replacement info lookup
description:
## What
When service is suspended, show where to catch the replacement shuttle bus. Curated static JSON lookup.

## Subtasks
- [ ] Create shuttle-stops.json: keyed by {lineId, fromStopId, toStopId} → shuttle stop descriptions
- [ ] Start with 10 most common suspension segments (research from MTA alert history)
- [ ] shuttle-matcher.ts: when alert has effect=NO_SERVICE, match against shuttle lookup
- [ ] Attach shuttle info to StationAlert response when matched
- [ ] Frontend: ShuttleInfo component showing bus stop locations and frequency
- [ ] Display inline within the alert card on AlertsScreen and AlertBanner
- [ ] Grow the lookup incrementally from production alert data

## Considerations
- MTA tends to suspend the same segments repeatedly — limited set covers most cases
- Shuttle bus stop locations are physical locations, not GTFS stops — use street descriptions
- Community reporting could supplement the static lookup in the future

title: Data freshness indicator per line
description:
## What
Show how old each line's data is on station detail views. Color-coded staleness.

## Subtasks
- [ ] Add feedName and feedAge fields to ArrivalTime responses from backend
- [ ] FreshnessFooter component: compact display of per-feed ages
- [ ] Color coding: green (<15s), neutral (15-45s), amber (45-90s), red (>90s)
- [ ] FreshnessDetail: expandable panel showing all 8 feed statuses
- [ ] Warning tint on arrivals when their feed is stale (>45s)
- [ ] Gray out arrivals when feed is very stale (>90s) with "(data may be outdated)"
- [ ] Integrate into system health dashboard (Phase 6) as "Data Health" section
close_reason: Completed data freshness indicator feature. All 7 subtasks were already implemented; wired DataHealth into HealthScreen.

title: Cross-Cutting: Testing, Security, Migration, Observability
description:
## Goal
Foundational infrastructure that spans all phases: testing strategy, security model, data migration, and observability. These are not features users see directly but they determine whether the app is trustworthy, maintainable, and operable.

## Why This Is Priority 1
These concerns must be addressed from the start, not retrofitted:
- Testing: feed snapshot fixtures must be recorded during Phase 1 development
- Security: rate limiting and CSP headers must be in place before public launch
- Data migration: Zustand versioned persist must be set up with the first store
- Observability: structured logging must be in the feed pipeline from day one

## Key Decisions
- No external monitoring infrastructure (no Prometheus, no Grafana) — structured JSON logs + rich /api/health endpoint
- Zod for all API input validation
- Cloudflare WAF + Hono token bucket for rate limiting (defense in depth)
- Feed snapshot testing with recorded binary fixtures (never depend on live MTA feeds in CI)
- Zustand's built-in version + migrate for localStorage schema evolution

## Acceptance Criteria
- Feed snapshot test fixtures recorded for all 8 feeds + alerts
- Unit, integration, and E2E test layers implemented and passing in CI
- Rate limiting active at both Cloudflare and application layers
- CSP headers served on all HTML responses
- Zod validation on all API endpoints
- Zustand stores use versioned persistence with backup migration safety
- /api/health returns comprehensive per-feed observability payload
- Structured JSON logging on every feed poll

title: Feed snapshot test fixtures and unit test foundation
description:
## What
Record actual MTA feed responses as binary test fixtures. Build unit test foundation for feed pipeline.

## Why
The feed pipeline is the heart of the app. It must be thoroughly tested against real data, but CI cannot depend on live MTA feeds. Snapshot testing is the solution.

## Subtasks
- [ ] Download and save one binary response per feed (8 subway + 1 alerts) as test fixtures
- [ ] Record edge cases: empty feed, feed with unassigned trips, feed with NYCT extensions
- [ ] Unit tests for parser.ts: parse each fixture, assert entity counts and field presence
- [ ] Unit tests for transformer.ts: transform parsed data, assert StationArrivals shape
- [ ] Unit tests for confidence.ts: all combinations of division + assigned → correct level
- [ ] Unit tests for alert-simplifier: each pattern in alert-patterns.json with fixture inputs
- [ ] Unit tests for delay-detector: mock position sequences, verify alert generation
- [ ] Zustand store migration tests: fixtures of each old schema, verify migration output
- [ ] Integration tests: in-memory Hono with fixture data, validate API response shapes via Zod

## Considerations
- Fixtures should be committed to git (binary files, ~50KB each)
- Re-record fixtures periodically to catch MTA feed format changes
- Tests must run fast (<10s for full unit suite)

title: Security: rate limiting, CSP, Zod validation
description:
## What
Implement the security model: Cloudflare WAF rules, Hono rate limiting middleware, Zod input validation, CSP headers.

## Subtasks
- [ ] Cloudflare WAF: rate-limit rule for /api/* at 100 req/min per IP (configure in tunnel dashboard)
- [ ] Hono middleware: in-memory token bucket rate limiter (60 req/min per IP)
- [ ] Zod schemas for all API inputs: push subscription payload, commute analysis request
- [ ] Return 400 with structured error for invalid payloads
- [ ] CSP headers: default-src 'self', script-src 'self', style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'
- [ ] Security headers: X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy
- [ ] Trip share link TTL: 24h expiry enforced server-side
- [ ] Audit: verify no PII stored server-side (push subs keyed by hash, no user identity)

## Considerations
- In-memory rate limiter resets on pod restart — acceptable for single-container deployment
- Cloudflare WAF is the first line of defense; Hono middleware is the second
- Zod schemas should be shared with frontend for consistent validation

title: Observability: structured logging + rich health endpoint
description:
## What
Structured JSON logging on every feed poll. Rich /api/health endpoint as observability dashboard.

## Subtasks
- [ ] Structured poll logging: JSON line per poll to stdout ({ts, feed, status, latencyMs, entities, parseErrors})
- [ ] Aggregate per-feed metrics: last poll time, consecutive failures, 24h error count, avg latency
- [ ] Rich /api/health response: all feeds status + alerts count + push subscription count + cache hit rate + memory
- [ ] /api/health returns 503 when 3+ feeds failing >5 min (triggers K8s readiness probe restart)
- [ ] Alert simplification match rate tracking: log matched vs unmatched alerts
- [ ] /status page: human-readable rendering of health endpoint (Phase 4 deliverable)

## Considerations
- Structured JSON to stdout is captured by Kubernetes logging — no log aggregation infrastructure needed
- The health endpoint serves double duty: K8s probes AND operator dashboard
- No Prometheus, no Grafana — keep it simple for a single-container app
close_reason: All observability features implemented in commit efbd44a. Structured JSON logging on every poll, per-feed metrics with latency/error tracking, rich /api/health endpoint with feeds/alerts/delayDetector/equipment/pushSubscriptions/cacheHitRate/memory, 503 on 3+ feeds failing >5min, alert match rate tracking. /status page deferred to Phase 4.

title: Phase 3: Alerts & Notifications - Alert simplification, push notifications
description: Implement alert system: plain-language alert simplification, station-based filtering, and push notification delivery.

title: Create commute journal
description: Build journal feature that records commute history including routes taken, actual vs. scheduled times, delays experienced, and notes. Provide summary views and patterns analysis over time.

title: Implement favorites management
description: Allow users to save favorite stations for quick access. Include add/remove favorites and persist across sessions.

title: Implement security hardening
description: Implement security hardening measures across the application. This includes input validation, output encoding, authentication/authorization improvements, secure headers, and protection against common vulnerabilities (OWASP Top 10).

title: Optimize performance
description: Implement code splitting, add lazy loading for images and components, analyze and reduce bundle size, and add appropriate caching headers. Acceptance: Lighthouse score >90, initial bundle under 200KB, and Time to Interactive <3s.

title: Build trip tracking with ETA
description: Create real-time trip tracking that shows current position along route and estimated time of arrival. Display progress visualization and update ETA based on live conditions.

title: Implement data migration tooling
description: Implement data migration tooling to handle schema changes and data transformations between versions. This should include versioned migration scripts, rollback capabilities, and safe execution patterns.

title: Implement observability infrastructure
description: Implement observability infrastructure including structured logging, metrics collection, and distributed tracing. Set up appropriate log levels, metric labels, and trace propagation to enable monitoring and debugging.

title: Improve accessibility (WCAG compliance)
description: Add ARIA labels, ensure keyboard navigation works throughout, verify screen reader compatibility, and fix color contrast issues. Acceptance: WCAG AA compliance, all interactive elements keyboard-accessible, and screen reader tests pass.
